title: Energy Optimisation in Metal Fabrication
summary: Analyzed spindle-level manufacturing energy data, identified seasonal demand patterns, and used time-series forecasting to propose a more data-driven operating model for a CNC screws plant.
highlights: Identified seasonality and non-linearity in spindle-level data from a CNC screws plant.
Applied Prophet for time-series forecasting and reached 1.06 percent MAPE.
Designed an IoT-oriented data-collection concept tied to real plant behavior.

sections.heading: Manufacturing problem
body: The project addressed energy optimization in a metal-fabrication environment by working from spindle-level plant data. Rather than treating power usage as a flat operational overhead, the work looked for patterns that could be modeled and anticipated.
That made the effort both analytical and operational: the point was to turn observed energy behavior into better planning and monitoring decisions.

sections.heading: Pattern discovery
body: A key part of the analysis was identifying seasonality and non-linearity in the manufacturing data. The observed behavior was linked to local auto-manufacturer order patterns, which reframed the plant's energy demand as something coupled to upstream business cycles.
This interpretation mattered because it pushed the project beyond pure curve fitting toward a more systems-aware explanation of plant load.

sections.heading: Forecasting approach
body: Prophet was used as the forecasting model, leading to a reported 1.06 percent Mean Absolute Percent Error. The low error made the model useful as an operational forecasting tool rather than just an academic comparison exercise.
By forecasting energy behavior over time, the work supported the broader goal of scheduling, monitoring, and process visibility.

sections.heading: Instrumentation outlook
body: The project also proposed an IoT-based data-collection layer, which is important because better models depend on better future measurements. That decision shows the work was not limited to analysis alone; it also considered how the data pipeline should improve.
The overall result was a manufacturing-energy case study that connected analytics, plant operations, and instrumentation design.
